# Literature Review {#sec-literature}

```{r setup, file = "R/chapter_start.R", include = FALSE}
# a number of commands need to run at the beginning of each chapter. This
# includes loading libraries that I always use, as well as options for 
# displaying numbers and text.
library(mlogit)
library(ggnewscale)
```

The nutrition access literature is long and has been approached from numerous
angles including public health, urban science and economics, and social justice.
In general, researchers have sought to link spatial disparities in access to nutrition 
resources with observed spatial disparities in economic resources or health indicators including obesity rates, 
caloric intake, and other outcomes. 
Complete --- though somewhat dated --- reviews of this literature can be had
from @beaulac2009 and @walker2010. @park2025 present a more recent systematic review of this literature, 
aided by an large language model-informed analysis of more than 800 articles; this more recent review 
emphasizes the wide variety of measures and methods that have been applied to the problem, with
a focus on differences between global regions. 

The existing nutrition environment literature has been somewhat inconsistent in its 
application of spatial or travel impedance units. @charreire2010 provides a categorization of
measures in the literature to that point: almost all studies used Euclidean or
occasionally network distance buffers, most frequently of one kilometer; 
a few studies used network travel time by an assumed travel mode, kernel density [@moore2008], or spatial clustering. 
In the last few years, a number of studies have attempted to explore more complex measures of
spatial access.
@cetateanu2016 and @widener2018 each pursue measures of time and space described by a
person's observed activity pattern and use it to explain food retail and purchasing
behaviors. These measures have not entered policy analysis at scale, however. 
Public health agencies in the United States continue to use Euclidean distance
to the nearest store as the basis for policy analysis [@rhone2017; @economicresearchservice2025].
When using travel times, the literature most often selects a single mode of travel, usually car or 
walking [@bennion2022]. When researchers consider multiple modes, it is usually done as a comparison [@burns2007; @kostopoulou2024]
or as multiple independent covariates in a large model [@murphy2017] rather than as a combined measure. 
This difference in methodological approaches matters substantially: @jaskiewicz2016 found little agreement
in definitions of good and poor food access between six different measures of access in Chicago.

Measures of the nutrition environment beyond spatial proximity are similarly inconsistent, though some validated methods have 
been developed [@baker2024]. A primary obstacle in developing such measures is the complexity
of the nutrition environment; many people obtain food from widely varying sources including convenience 
stores, supermarkets, small grocery stores, and restaurants of all kinds [@ansai2023]. 
Non-spatial measures of this environment are important for policy analysis given that many people do not shop
at their nearest store alone [@aggarwal2014], and that the quality of offerings at stores
may explain obesity rates as much as spatial access to stores  [@cooksey-stowers2017; @chen2016].
Foremost among the validated measures of the nutrition environment is the NEMS-S
framework [@glanz2007; @glanz2023], in which trained surveyors record the
presence, price, and shelf space allocated to various food items in stores. 
But even the NEMS-S is not entirely comprehensive: separate instruments exist for stores (NEMS-S),
corner stores (NEMS-CS), and restaurants (NEMS-R), vending machines (NEMS-V), and quick service prepared food stores (NEMS-GG). 
Applying one measure to the entire spatial environment is not a viable approach, and may result in
discontinuities. As a result of these discontinuities, researchers frequently
segment the environment, asserting which stores are to be included and which are
to be excluded. For example, @widener2018 develop a complex time-and-activity space measure 
respondent's spatial proximity, but apply this measure to discrete segments of
grocery stores, convenience stores, etc. rather than to a holistic picture of the nutrition environment.
More simplistic measures of spatial access --- e.g., Euclidean distance to nearest store --- require researchers to assert what 
they will include and what they will exclude. 


What has not been pursued in the literature to this point is an approach that
considers how the multi-modal travel behavior of individuals with respect to 
transportation infrastructure shapes spatial access to nutrition resources
*as well as* the availability and quality of foods at the relevant destinations. Such an
approach has been shown to improve the correlation between spatial disparities in health
outcomes and access to urban green spaces [@macfarlane2021modeling] and to better inform
parks-related policy analysis [@macfarlane2022]. It is this combination that we pursue in this study.
